note:
我的书《友好的橙色光芒：柏拉图体系的不为人知的故事与网络文化的黎明》(The Friendly Orange Glow：The Untaled Story of the Plato System and the曙光)一书中有一个从未出版过的章节。这本书是为了庆祝伊利诺伊大学柏拉图计算机教育系统启动60周年，1960年6月第一周启动的一个计算机项目，也是为了纪念已故的罗杰·埃伯特(Roger Ebert)，他是著名的电影评论家和柏拉图的粉丝，也是教育家约翰·亨特(John Hunter)，他同时遇到了埃伯特。。。
约翰·亨特觉得自己被明星迷住了，被雷霆迷住了。他是一个面带微笑的解除武装的人，他描述自己是一名来自弗吉尼亚州阿尔伯马尔县的“小镇学校教师”，他喜欢教孩子们，这是他自20世纪70年代末以来一直在做的事情。2011年3月4日，星期五，亨特在加利福尼亚州的长滩，在他想不到的最后一个地方：在TED会议的后台，那个昂贵得令人望而却步的名人和伟大思想的年度聚会。
TED的组织者听说过一部获奖纪录片，世界和平游戏，讲述了亨特和他的四年级学生的故事，突然他发现自己今天在这里，穿着一件令人惊叹的明亮的多色短袖衬衫和黑色裤子，一个微型的头戴式无线麦克风，脸上抹着一点化妆品，而他正在等待成为会议的倒数第二位演讲者。只是参加TED会议是一回事，但对于他来说，被邀请在这么多成就卓著的医生、音乐家、技术员、工程师、百万富翁、亿万富翁、作家、大厨、艺术家、科学家、历史学家、未来学家、企业巨头、古生物学家、诗人、军事指挥官、摄影师、物理学家、电影制作人和生物学家中演讲是一回事！大多数人已经攀登到了他们职业生涯的金字塔顶端。一群如此杰出的人，几乎令人费解。对于亨特来说，这种经历感觉就像“NASA和好莱坞走到了一起”，“去了另一个星球”。
在等待的过程中，他看到了会议的最后一位演讲者，传奇的普利策奖获得者、影评人罗杰·埃伯特(Roger Ebert)，他多年来一直处于电影业金字塔顶端，与妻子查兹(Chaz)静静地躺在附近的沙发上。
亨特和埃伯特进行眼神交流。这是亨特第一次见到罗杰·埃伯特本人。什么也没说。
然后就是时候了。亨特走了出去，发表了他的演讲。(你可以在这个YouTube页面上观看这次演讲的视频。)。
“我很幸运能站在这里，”他在铺着红地毯的圆形舞台上说。他告诉观众，他打电话给他的妻子，向她描述TED会议是一个“有如此多的好人试图做这么多好事的地方，感觉就像我降落在天使的聚居地。”
“它是这样开始的，”他说，描述了他发明的这款引起了如此多国际关注的游戏。“这只是一块4英尺乘5英尺的胶合板，在市中心的一所城市学校里，1978年。我正在为学生们制作一堂关于非洲的课。我们把世界上所有的问题都放在那里，我想，让他们来解决吧。我不想讲课，也不想只看书。我想让他们沉浸其中，学习通过身体学习的感觉。“
多年来，游戏从单一的胶合板演变为四英尺乘四英尺、四英尺乘四英尺的多层有机玻璃结构，最顶层代表天空和空间；下一层代表地面，包括城市、工厂、农场、山脉、湖泊、田野和森林；另一层代表大海；底层代表海底发生的事情。没有电源线。没有电脑为这个提供动力。这一切都是简单的，物理的，手工建造的。他说：“孩子们编造了国家的名字--有些是富的，有些是贫穷的。”“他们拥有不同的资产，商业和军事资产。而且每个国家都有内阁。“。孩子们扮演总理、国务卿、国防部长和财政部长的角色。“我是根据我与他们的关系来选择总理的。我给他们这份工作，他们可以拒绝，然后他们就可以选择自己的内阁。“。
在接下来的15分钟里，亨特用关于他的世界和平游戏对几代孩子产生的影响的故事和轶事迷住了观众。在他讲话时，上方的一段大型视频显示了纪录片的片段，展示了孩子们如何以承诺、专注、激情和不可思议的聪明才智投入到自己的角色中，巧妙地应对战争、金融危机、政治问题和与其他国家的贸易谈判。
亨特描述了其他游戏情况，以及孩子们是如何管理它们的，以及在这个过程中，他们学到的智慧将能够伴随他们的余生。“我们每次比赛都是不同的，”他说。“有些游戏更多的是关于社会问题，有些游戏更多的是关于经济问题。有些游戏更多的是关于战争的。但我并不想否认他们作为人类的现实。我允许他们去那里，通过他们自己的经历，以一种不流血的方式学习如何不做他们认为是错误的事情。他们用他们自己的方式，他们自己找出什么是正确的。“。
然后就结束了。起立鼓掌。当观众鼓掌时，他像在祈祷一样把手掌放在一起，向观众中的一部分鞠躬，然后转向另一部分观众再次鞠躬，再次转身向更多的人鞠躬，然后走下舞台。很快，TED的工作人员冲上舞台，将黑色皮椅排成一排，摆在前面和中心，为下一次演讲做好准备。
在后台，亨特看到罗杰·埃伯特不再静静地坐着。他起来了。他正在为即将开始的他自己的演讲做准备。他兴奋，热情，面带微笑。亨特注意到他在做手势。对他来说。罗杰·埃伯特在向他打手势。表明他想让亨特出现在舞台上的手势。和他在一起。现在就来。
埃伯特的健康状况永久性地夺去了他的声音，他准备了一个剧本，他还挑选了另外两个人为他朗读：他的妻子查兹，以及TED的常客、著名的临床医学教授迪恩·奥尼什(Dean Orish)。在没有任何警告的情况下，罗杰·埃伯特现在向小镇的学校教师约翰·亨特发出信号，让他和他们三人一起上台，也朗读罗杰的一部分剧本。埃伯特、他的妻子和奥尼什已经准备好要出去了。TED的人们，就像亨特一样，对这个出人意料的议程变化毫无准备，争先恐后地把第四把椅子搬上舞台，与其他椅子排成一排。埃伯特让他的助手拿来另一份剧本。他把它拿给亨特看，指给亨特看他应该读的部分。
就这样。这就是全部的准备工作。现在亨特就要回到舞台上了。有时候，生活有一种自己制定计划的方式。亨特似乎被他无法控制的力量席卷而去。
他们四个走到舞台上，坐在椅子上。观众热情地向他们打招呼。
埃伯特穿着白色裤子，黑色衬衫，黑色夹克，脖子和脸底小心翼翼地裹着一块黑布，以掩盖下面缺失的东西。他多年来一直在与癌症作斗争，他所经历的手术引起了不和谐的变化：医生不得不切除他的下巴，留下了下巴的肉，没有任何骨头支撑。没有下巴，他的嘴巴是永久张开的，他的脸是永久惊讶的表情。
在数千名与会者聚集在礼堂并通过远程广播观看之前，埃伯特即将在为期一周的紧张TED会议的最后一天的闭幕会议上做最后一组演讲者的最后一次演讲。他的腿上放着一台敞开的笔记本电脑。
突然，观众听到礼堂的扩音器里传来了埃伯特的话，但他张开的嘴巴没有说出来。“这些是我说的话，但这不是我的声音，”他笔记本电脑上的音频信号说。“这是亚历克斯，我所能找到的最好的电脑语音，它是每台麦金塔电脑上的标准设备。”
他右边坐着他的妻子查兹。在他的左边坐着亨特，在亨特的左边坐着奥尼什。每个人手中都拿着一个剧本，每个人都跟着Mac电脑继续说罗杰的话。“我已经决定招募我的一些TED朋友来为我朗读我的话，”这个无实体的声音说，埃伯特解释说，他觉得用电脑声音朗读他18分钟的演讲对观众来说太无聊了。当那个声音这样说的时候，埃伯特把双手放在一起，把头以一定的角度放在上面，就像他要睡觉一样。观众咯咯笑了起来。
然后查兹开始朗读剧本的下一段，讲述了罗杰对试图恢复他说话能力的医疗手术的悲惨描述，但这只会让事情变得更糟。在查斯继续阅读的过程中，热情洋溢的罗杰可能是为了减轻礼堂的情绪，也许只是为了做他自己，他兴高采烈地默剧着，做着手势，指着这里或那里，竖起两个大拇指，扬起眉毛，一度给了查兹一个知情的眼神，导致她停了下来，回头看了看，因为观众有点不安地笑了起来。
在查兹演讲几分钟后，迪恩·奥尼什和约翰·亨特轮流继续罗杰的演讲，不时停下来播放简短的视频剪辑或音频演示，其中一个完全令人毛骨悚然：罗杰展示了计算机生成的文本到语音转换的最新进展，这个声音听起来几乎与数百万电视节目观众熟悉的著名的埃伯特的声音一模一样。
在约翰·亨特(John Hunter)的剧本片段中，观众听到了从地球地质历史的非常长的跨度来看，在如此短的微观时间内，技术进步了多少。“在它几百万年、几十亿年的几乎所有时间里，地球上根本没有生命。在地球上几乎所有的生命年头里，都没有智慧生命。只有在我们学会了将知识代代相传之后，文明才成为可能。用宇宙学的术语来说，那是大约十分钟前的事了。最后，人类最先进、最神秘的工具--计算机问世了。这在我的有生之年几乎都发生过。“。
亨特继续读下去。“一些著名的早期计算机是在我的家乡厄巴纳建造的，那里是HAL-9000(电影”2001：太空漫游“中的计算机)的诞生地。周三，当我听到萨尔曼·汗(Salman Khan)关于可汗学院(Khan Academy)网站的令人惊叹的演讲时，他向世界各地的学生讲授了数百门课程，我突然想起了这一点。“
萨尔曼·汗(Salman Khan)两天前的TED演讲的题目是“让我们用视频重塑教育。”这呼应了一种情绪，其他许多人，几十年来，可能并不知道可汗之前就表达过这种情绪。对于可汗来说，这个想法是他在波士顿担任对冲基金分析师期间试图远程辅导他在新奥尔良的年轻堂兄弟们的结果。“我开始上传第一个YouTube视频，真的很不错，只是对我的堂兄弟们的补充，”汗说，并继续说：
当我把第一个YouTube视频放到网上时，一些有趣的事情发生了--实际上发生了很多有趣的事情。他们告诉我，他们更喜欢我在YouTube上，而不是面对面。一旦你克服了这种反手的本质，实际上是有一些非常深刻的东西。他们说，比起他们的表弟，他们更喜欢自动化版的表弟！“。他们喜欢的是，他们可以在自己方便的时候观看视频，暂停视频或重新播放视频片段。按照他们自己的步调。他的另一个发现是，其他完全陌生的人开始观看这些YouTube视频。他们开始评论他们。越来越多的陌生人开始观看并发表评论。好评如潮。这些视频正在帮助人们。。。我当时是一家对冲基金的分析师。对我来说，做一些有社会价值的事情是非常奇怪的。
他制作了更多的视频。更多的人观看了他们的节目。因此，他在不同的科目上取得了更多的成就。在接下来的几年里，这个项目发展到了他成立了KhanAcademy的地步，有数以千计的视频和数百万人观看并从中学习。萨尔曼·汗(Salman Khan)在不知不觉中成为了迅速为人所知的MOOC的先驱：大规模在线公开课。
在他的演讲期间，TED舞台上方的屏幕上播放了视频剪辑和互动学习展示，展示了一些课程和测验的例子，这些课程和测验现在全世界有数百万名学生可以利用他的可汗学院(Khan Academy)。他展示的一些课程例子是简单的算术，在网络浏览器页面(装饰着可汗学院的品牌、徽标，以及在麦当劳式的“提供数十亿次服务”的计数中，“提供的课程”的数量不断增加)中显示了一系列减法问题，如“3-1=？”和“8-2=？”和“7-7=？”在屏幕的最右边是一个框，学生可以在框中输入她的答案，然后收到关于该答案的是/否反馈。“这里的范例是，你需要多少问题，它就会产生多少问题，”可汗解释说，“直到你得到那个概念。”
他谈到鼓励学生掌握连续的知识，从而最终掌握。要坚持下去，直到他们得到它，而不是担心其他人做得有多好-学习是你在内心做的事情，按照你自己的节奏：
所以当你谈论自定进度的学习时，它对每个人来说都是有意义的-从教育的角度讲，差异化学习-但当你在课堂上看到它时，它就有点疯狂了。因为每次我们这样做，在我们做过的每个教室里，一遍又一遍，如果你进入五天，会有一群孩子跑在前面，有一群孩子稍微慢一点。在传统的模型中，如果你做一个快照评估，你会说，“这些是有天赋的孩子，这些是反应迟钝的孩子。也许应该以不同的方式追踪他们。也许我们应该把他们分到不同的班级。“。但是，当你让每个学生按照自己的节奏学习时-我们一遍又一遍地看到-你会看到学生在一个或另一个概念上花了一点额外的时间，但一旦他们通过了那个概念，他们就会跑在前面。所以六周前你认为迟钝的孩子，现在你会认为他们很有天赋。我们一遍又一遍地看到它。这让你真的想知道，也许我们中的许多人从这些标签中受益的程度，实际上是因为时间的巧合。
Khan是一家对冲基金的分析师，他发现了自定进度、个性化学习的好处，以及立即向学习者反馈的好处。在过去的一百年里，这是许多人一直在推动的两个大想法，他们以各种形式在教室里实验，并使用机械和电子设备。目前还不清楚Khanor的观众是否意识到了这一点。但在那个周三的2011年TED大会上，这并不重要。观众喜欢可汗的演讲，喜欢他的远见。他们把它吃光了。这显然就是未来。这显然是一种更好的方式。为什么不用可汗的视频来重塑教育呢？
埃伯特参加了可汗周三的TED演讲，在那期间，他经历了那次倒叙，回到很久以前，在他自己的职业生涯刚刚开始的时候，很久以前，在他成为一名电影评论家之前，当他还是一名十几岁的年轻记者时，他刚刚开始在他心爱的家乡奥克兰的“新闻公报”工作。
约翰·亨特继续阅读埃伯特事先准备好的讲稿。他描述了倒叙。“我被送到伊利诺伊大学的计算机实验室，”亨特说，“去采访一种叫做‘PLATO’的东西的创建者。”缩写代表用于自动教学操作的编程逻辑。这是一个计算机辅助教学系统。在那些日子里，它在一台名为ILLIAC的计算机上运行。程序员说，它可以帮助学生学习。我怀疑在50年前的那一天，他们甚至连萨勒曼·汗所取得的成就都没有梦想过。“。
埃伯特可能对此表示怀疑，但柏拉图的创造者们确实梦想过那种程度的成就，他们梦想着达到那样的规模。但是他们很早就开始了。甚至在可汗出生前的16年多。柏拉图开始于1960年，早在卡恩几年后能够免费利用的任何基础设施存在之前，甚至在这些基础设施被发明之前。1960年，最初的柏拉图团队别无选择，只能发明一种基础设施，因为没有任何基础设施可以利用。他们必须从头开始发明系统、通信、硬件、软件，所有的一切，每一步都要从头开始。他们受到充沛的、年轻的自信和对未来的愿景的指引，未来的愿景如此清晰，他们只需伸手去抓住它，对他们来说，这是显而易见的。
有多少TED与会者知道埃伯特在这里说的是什么？值得怀疑的不只是少数人。几年后，当我采访亨特时，我会知道，即使是他以前也没有听说过这句话。柏拉图？伊利阿克？这些都不是通用的术语，即使在主流的、规范的计算机历史的编年史上也不是。柏拉图和ILLIAC不是像Apple Macintosh、IBM PC、Xerox Alto或Google那样的传奇人物，它们的神话般的故事仍然在硅谷的技术精英中怀着崇敬和敬畏的心情讲述，或者在科技圣经、连线杂志的页面上讲述。柏拉图和ILLIAC也不是来自另一个计算机海岸的著名发明，马萨诸塞州剑桥市的麻省理工学院。
亨特继续看书。“柏拉图才五十年前。时间的一瞬间。直到五年前，它还在以这样或那样的形式在越来越复杂的计算机上继续发展和运行。我从维基百科上了解到，从那个卑微的开始，柏拉图建立了论坛、留言板、在线测试、电子邮件、聊天室、图片语言、即时消息、远程屏幕共享和多人游戏。由于第一个网络浏览器也是在厄巴纳开发的，看来我位于伊利诺伊州北部的家乡是我们今天所占据的大部分虚拟在线世界的发源地！
通过坐在他旁边的三个人的声音，埃伯特的演讲又持续了几分钟。最后，观众起立欢呼。非常长的一周，紧张的，扩张性的2011年TED大会演讲结束了。每个人都站起来去野餐，在外面等着。
“和他在一起是一次非常感人的经历。。。一个特殊的时刻，“亨特后来会告诉我，回想起他2011年与埃伯特的遭遇。“我猜TED策划了他们的演讲序列。非正式地，我听说他们在一些谈话中切中要害，在另一些谈话中切中要害，所以罗杰的谈话被刻意设计成非常情绪化的结束语。我的演讲在某种程度上是他演讲的一个片段。“。这是约翰·亨特第一次也是最后一次见到罗杰·埃伯特。
2013年4月4日，这种疾病终于夺走了埃伯特的生命。同一天，《纽约客》在其网站上发表了埃伯特去世前不久在医院病床上写的一篇未完成的短篇小说。
他一直在评论电影，直到最后，但他的妻子查兹认为，尝试一下其他的东西可能对他有好处。“我建议他从与工作相关的写作中抽身出来休息一下，”查兹说，“写一些有创意的东西，让他感觉自己小时候为粉丝写科幻文章时就是这样做的。”罗杰就是这么做的。由此产生的故事被命名为“泰坦的思维分子”，以三所大学的研究人员为开场白。

Here is a never-before-published chapter that didn’t make it into my book   The Friendly Orange Glow: The Untold Story of the PLATO System and the Dawn of Cyberculture, offered here in celebration of the 60th Anniversary of the start of the PLATO computer-based education system at the University of Illinois, a computer project launched the first week of June 1960, and also in celebration of the late Roger Ebert, the acclaimed film critic and fan of PLATO, and also educator John Hunter, who simultaneously met Ebert and learned about PLATO for the first time, in a most unusual and unexpected way. . .
   J ohn Hunter feels star-struck, thunderstruck. A disarming man with a warm smile, he describes himself as a “small-town schoolteacher” from Albemarle County, Virginia, who loves teaching kids, something he has been doing since the late 1970s. On Friday, March 4th, 2011, Hunter is in Long Beach, California, in the last place he ever thought he would find himself: backstage at a TED conference, that prohibitively expensive annual gathering of luminaries and big ideas.
 The TED organizers had heard about an award-winning documentary,  World Peace Game, that tells the story of Hunter and his fourth-grade students, and suddenly he finds himself here today, wearing an amazing, bright, multi-colored short-sleeve shirt and black pants, a tiny head-mounted wireless microphone, and a dab of makeup on his face, while he waits to go on as the conference’s penultimate speaker. It’s one thing just  attending a TED conference, but for him to be invited to be a  speaker, among so many accomplished doctors, musicians, technologists, engineers, millionaires, billionaires, writers, master chefs, artists, scientists, historians, futurists, corporate titans, paleontologists, poets, military commanders, photographers, physicists, filmmakers, and biologists! Most have climbed to the very top of their career’s pyramids. A group so distinguished, it’s nearly incomprehensible. For Hunter the experience feels like “NASA and Hollywood getting together” and “going to another planet.”
 While he’s waiting, he spots the conference’s final speaker, legendary Pulitzer Prize-winning film critic Roger Ebert, who for years has commanded the very  tip of the top of the movie industry’s pyramid, reclining quietly with his wife Chaz on a nearby couch.
 Hunter and Ebert make eye contact. It’s the first time Hunter has ever seen Roger Ebert in person. Nothing is said.
 And then it is time. Hunter goes out and gives his talk. (You can watch the video of this talk at this   YouTube page.)
  “I’m very fortunate to be here,” he says from the round, red-carpeted stage. He tells the audience that he called his wife and described the TED conference to her as a place where “there’s so many good people trying to do so much good, it feels like I’ve landed in a colony of angels.”
 “It started out like this,” he says, describing the game he invented that has gotten so much international attention. “It’s just a four-foot by five-foot plywood board in an inner-city urban school, 1978. I was creating a lesson for students on Africa. We put all the problems of the world there, and I thought, let’s let them solve it. I didn’t want to lecture or have just book reading. I wanted to have them be immersed and learn the feeling of learning through their bodies.”
 Over the years the game evolved from the single plywood board to a four-foot by four-foot by four-foot multi-level Plexiglas structure, the topmost layer representing the sky and space; the next layer representing the ground with its cities, factories, farms, mountains, lakes, fields, and forests; another layer for the sea; and a bottom layer for what goes on under the sea. There is no electrical cord. There’s no computer powering this. It’s all simple and physical and hand-built. “The kids make up the names of the countries — some are rich, some are poor,” he says. “They have different assets, commercial and military. And each country has a cabinet.” The kids play the roles of Prime Minister, Secretary of State, Minister of Defense, and a finance minister. “I choose the Prime Minister based on my relationship with them. I offer them the job, they can turn it down, and then they can choose their own cabinet.”
  For the next fifteen minutes, Hunter mesmerizes the audience with stories and anecdotes about the effect his  World Peace Game has had on generations of kids. While speaking, a large video display above him shows clips from the documentary film, showing how kids dive into their roles with commitment, focus, passion, and uncanny smarts, deftly juggling war, financial crises, political problems, and trade negotiations with other countries.
 Hunter describes other game situations and how the children managed them and in the process, learned wisdom they’ll be able to take with them for the rest of their lives. “Every time we play is different,” he says. “Some games are more about social issues, some are more about economic issues. Some games are more about warfare. But I don’t try to deny them that reality of being human. I allow them to go there and, through their own experience, learn, in a bloodless way, how not to do what they consider to be the wrong thing. And they find out what is right their own way, their own selves.”
 And then it is over. A standing ovation. While the audience claps, he presses the palms of his hands together as if in prayer and bows to one section of the audience, then turns to another and bows again, turning and bowing once more to yet more people, then walks off the stage. Soon, TED staff rush black leather chairs onto the stage and line them up front and center, ready for the next presentation.
 Backstage, Hunter sees that Roger Ebert is no longer quietly sitting. He’s up. He’s preparing for his own talk which is about to begin. He’s excited, enthusiastic, smiling. Making gestures, Hunter notices. To  him. Roger Ebert is gesturing to  him. Gestures that indicate he wants Hunter to come out on the stage. With  him.  Right now.
 Ebert, whose health condition had permanently taken away his voice, had prepared a script that he’d picked two others to read for him: his wife Chaz, and a TED regular, renowned clinical professor of medicine Dean Ornish. With no warning whatsoever, here is Roger Ebert now signaling small-town schoolteacher John Hunter to join the three of them onstage and read a portion of Roger’s script as well. Ebert, his wife, and Ornish are ready to go out. The TED people, as unprepared as Hunter is for this surprise change to the agenda, scramble to bring a fourth chair out to the stage and line it up with the others. Ebert has his assistant fetch another copy of the script. He shows it to Hunter, points out to Hunter the section he’s supposed to read.
 That’s it. That’s the entire preparation. Now Hunter’s about to go back on stage. Sometimes life has a way of making plans on its own. It’s as if Hunter is being swept along by forces beyond his control.
 The four of them go out to the stage and sit down in their chairs. The audience greets them warmly.
  Ebert wears white pants, a black shirt, a black jacket, and a black cloth carefully draped around his neck and the bottom of his face to conceal what is missing beneath. The cancer he’s been fighting for years and the surgeries he’s endured have caused jarring changes: doctors have had to remove his lower jaw, and it has left him with the flesh of a chin that lacks the support of any bone. Without a jaw his mouth is permanently open, his face an expression of permanent surprise.
  Before thousands of attendees gathered in the auditorium and looking on from remote broadcasts, Ebert is about to give the last presentation of the final group of speakers during the closing session on the last day of what had been an intense, week-long TED conference. On his lap is an open laptop computer.
  Suddenly the audience hears words come over the auditorium’s loudspeakers — Ebert’s words — but his open mouth is not uttering them. “These are my words but this is not my voice,” an audio signal from his laptop says. “This is Alex, the best computer voice I’ve been able to find, which comes as standard equipment on every Macintosh.”
 To his right sits his wife, Chaz. On his left sits Hunter and to Hunter’s left, Ornish. Each holds a script in their hands, each following along as the Mac computer continues speaking Roger’s words. “I’ve decided to recruit some of my TED friends to read my words aloud for me,” the disembodied voice says, Ebert explaining that he felt a computer voice reading his entire 18-minute presentation would be too boring for the audience. While the voice says this, Ebert places his hands together and rests his head on them at an angle as if he were about to go to sleep. The audience chuckles.
   Chaz then begins reading the next segment of the script, relating Roger’s harrowing account of the medical operations that attempted to restore his ability to speak, but only made matters worse. As Chas reads on, an ebullient Roger, perhaps attempting to lighten the mood of the auditorium, perhaps just being himself, cheerfully mimes along, gesturing, pointing here or there, offering two thumbs up, raising his eyebrows, and at one point giving Chaz a knowing look, causing her to pause and look back, as laughs arise from a slightly uneasy audience.
 After a few minutes of Chaz, Dean Ornish and John Hunter take turns continuing through Roger’s speech, here and there stopping to play brief video clips or audio demonstrations, one of which is downright eerie: Roger showing off a more recent advance in computer-generated text-to-speech, a voice sounding nearly identical to that famous Ebert voice familiar to millions of his TV show’s viewers.
 In John Hunter’s script segment, the audience hears how far technology has advanced in such a microscopically short time, when viewed from the very long span of Earth’s geologic history. “For almost all of its millions and billions of years, there was no life on Earth at all. For almost all of the years of life on Earth, there was no intelligent life. Only after we learned to pass knowledge from one generation to the next did civilization become possible. In cosmological terms, that was about ten minutes ago. Finally, came mankind’s most advanced and mysterious tool, the computer. That has mostly happened in my lifetime.”
 Hunter reads on. “Some of the famous early computers were being built in my home town of Urbana, the birthplace of HAL-9000 [the computer from the film  2001: A Space Odyssey]. When I heard the amazing talk by Salman Khan on Wednesday, about the Khan Academy website that teaches hundreds of subjects to students all over the world, I had a flashback.”
  Salman Khan’s TED talk two days earlier had been entitled, “ Let’s Use Video to Reinvent Education.” It echoed a sentiment that others, many others, over many decades, have expressed before, probably unbeknownst to Khan. For Khan, the notion came to him as a result of trying to remotely tutor his young cousins in New Orleans while he was working as a hedge fund analyst in Boston. “I started putting the first YouTube videos up really just as kind a nice-to-have, just a supplement for my cousins,” Khan said, continuing:
 As soon as I put those first YouTube videos up, something interesting happened — actually a bunch of interesting things happened. They told me they preferred me on YouTube than in person. And once you get over the backhanded nature of that, there was actually something very profound there. They were saying that they preferred the automated version of their cousin to their cousin!” They liked that they could watch the videos when they wanted, pause them or re-play sections of them at their own convenience. At their own  pace. His other discovery was that  other people, total strangers, started watching these YouTube videos. And they started commenting on them. More and more strangers started watching and offering comments.  Positive comments. The videos were helping people . . . Here I was an analyst at a hedge fund. It was very strange for me to be doing something of social value.
 He made more videos. More people watched them. So he made even more, in varying subjects. Over the next couple of years, the project grew to the point where he formed the KhanAcademy, with thousands of videos and millions of people watching and learning from them. Salman Khan had unwittingly become a pioneer in what quickly became known as MOOCs: massive online open courses.
 All during his talk, clips of videos and interactive learning displays ran on the screen above the TED stage, showing examples of some of the lessons and quizzes available to the now millions of students worldwide who take advantage of his Khan Academy. Some of the lesson examples he showed are simple arithmetic, displaying in a web browser page (festooned with the Khan Academy brand, logo, and, in a McDonald’s-like “billions served” count, the ever-growing number of “lessons delivered”), a series of subtraction problems, like “3 -1 = ?” and “8 - 2 = ?” and “7 - 7 = ?” and on the far right of the screen, a box into which the student would type in her answer and then receive yes/no feedback on that answer. “The paradigm here is that it will generate as many questions as you need,” Khan explained, “until you get that concept.”
 He spoke of encouraging students to master successive bits of knowledge leading to eventual mastery. To keep at it until they get it, not worrying about how well anyone else is doing — learning is something you do  inside, at your own pace:
 So when you talk about self-paced learning, it makes sense for everyone — in education-speak, differentiated learning — but it’s kind of crazy when you see it in a classroom. Because every time we’ve done this, in every classroom we’ve done, over and over again, if you go five days into it, there’s a group of kids who’ve raced ahead and there’s a group of kids who are a little bit slower. And in a traditional model, if you did a snapshot assessment, you say, “These are the gifted kids, these are the slow kids. Maybe they should be tracked differently. Maybe we should put them in different classes.” But when you let every student work at their own pace — and we see it over and over and over again — you see students who took a little bit [of] extra time on one concept or the other, but once they get through that concept, they just race ahead. And so the same kids that you thought were slow six weeks ago, you now would think are gifted. And we’re seeing it over and over and over again. And it makes you really wonder how much all of the labels maybe a lot of us have benefited from were really just due to a coincidence of time.
 Khan, an analyst at a hedge fund, had discovered the benefits of self-paced, individualized learning, along with the benefits of immediate feedback to the learner. These are two Big Ideas that a lot of people have been pushing for, experimenting with in various forms, in classrooms and using devices both mechanical and electronic, for the last  one hundred years. It is not clear if Khanor the audience realized this. But that did not matter that Wednesday at the 2011 TED conference. The audience loved Khan’s talk, his vision. They ate it up. This was clearly the future. This was so  obviously a better way to go. Why  not use Khan’s videos to reinvent education?
 Ebert had attended Khan’s Wednesday TED talk, and during it he had experienced that flashback, back to a time, long ago, at the dawn of his own career, long before he became a film critic, back when he was just starting out as a teenage cub reporter for the  News-Gazette newspaper in his beloved hometown of Urbana, Illinois.
  John Hunter continues reading Ebert’s prepared remarks. He describes the flashback. “I was sent over to the computer lab of the University of Illinois,” Hunter says, “to interview the creators of something called ‘ PLATO.’ The initials stood for Programmed Logic for Automatic Teaching Operations. This was a computer-assisted instruction system. Which in those days ran on a computer named ILLIAC. The programmers said it could assist students in their learning. I doubt on that day 50 years ago they even dreamed of what Salman Khan has accomplished.”
  Ebert may have doubted it, but PLATO’s creators  did dream of that level of accomplishment, and they dreamed of that  scale. But they were starting early. More than sixteen years before Khan was even born. PLATO began in 1960, long before any of the infrastructure that Kahn would years later be able to take advantage of for free, existed or had even been invented. In 1960 the original PLATO team had no choice but to invent an infrastructure because there was none to take advantage of. They had to invent systems, communications, hardware, software, everything, all of it from scratch, every step of the way. They were guided by their abundant, youthful self-confidence and by a vision of the future so crisp that they could simply reach out and grab it, its achievability to them obvious.
 How many TED attendees have any idea what Ebert’s talking about here? It’s doubtful more than a handful. I will learn years later when I interview Hunter that not even he had heard of these words before.  PLATO? ILLIAC? These are not common terms, not even in the annals of mainstream, canonical computer history. PLATO and ILLIAC are not legends like the Apple Macintosh, the IBM PC, or the Xerox Alto, or Google, whose tales of mythic proportions are still told with reverence and awe among the technorati of Silicon Valley, or in the pages of the technorati bible,  WIRED magazine. Neither are PLATO and ILLIAC famous inventions from that  other computer coast, MIT in Cambridge, Massachusetts.
 Hunter continues reading. “PLATO was only fifty years ago. An  instant in time. It continued to evolve and operated in one form or another on more and more sophisticated computers until only five years ago. I have learned from Wikipedia, that starting from that humble beginning, PLATO established forums, message boards, online testing, email, chat rooms, picture languages, instant messaging, remote screen sharing, and multiple player games. Since the first web browser was also developed in Urbana, it appears that my home town in downstate Illinois was the birthplace of much of the virtual online universe we occupy today!”
 Ebert’s speech continues for several more minutes through the voices of the three seated next to him. At the end, the audience stands and cheers. The very long week of intense, mind-expanding 2011 TED conference presentations is over. Everyone gets up and heads to the picnic awaiting outside.
 “It was a very moving experience to be out there with him . . . a special moment in time,” Hunter would tell me later, thinking back on his 2011 encounter with Ebert. “I gather that TED orchestrates their talk sequence. Unofficially I heard they were appealing to the head in some talks and the heart in other talks, so that Roger’s talk was calculated to be the very emotional closing that it was. My talk was in a way a segue into his talk.” It was the first and last time John Hunter would ever see Roger Ebert.
  On April 4, 2013, the illness would finally take Ebert’s life. On that same day, the  New Yorker published on its website an unfinished short story that Ebert had written in a hospital bed shortly before he passed away.
 He was reviewing films right up until the very end, but his wife Chaz thought it might be good for him to try something else for a while. “I suggested that he take a break from work-related writing,” Chaz says, “and write something creative that made him feel like he did when he was writing science fiction articles for fanzines when he was a boy.” And so Roger did. The resulting story was called “  The Thinking Molecules of Titan,” which opens with three university r

今天我醒来时听到了一个令人悲伤和震惊的消息，乔恩·道林(主页/Twitter/维基百科)-路易斯安那州立大学的物理学教授，早在90年代就让美国政府投资量子计算的人，畅销书《薛定谔的杀手应用：竞相建造世界上第一台量子计算机》的作者，BosonSamples的调查者，以及其他许多话题的研究者，拥有一个“量子比特”车牌，也是我在量子计算幽默领域的主要竞争对手之一。
三个月前，就在Covid关闭世界之前，我做的最后一次旅行是从奥斯汀到巴吞鲁日，和我的博士后安德里亚·罗切托(Andrea Rocchetto)一起，从奥斯汀到巴吞鲁日，花了7个小时的公路旅行，在路易斯安那州立大学物理系发表了一场名为赫恩讲座(The Hearne Lecture)的演讲。我的话题(不出所料)是谷歌的量子至上实验。
我曾考虑过是否取消这次旅行，因为坐飞机似乎已经太危险了。道林是那个说“为什么不带着你的博士后驾车来这里呢？”--这对我和安德里亚来说是一次难忘的经历，结束了对LIGO的私人游览，并参观了一个鳄鱼宝宝孵化场。我不知道这是我最后一次见到乔恩·道林，但现在我非常高兴我们这次来访。
在我演讲后的晚宴上，道林和我每次见到他时一模一样：吵闹、醉醺醺的、令人讨厌的，而且滑稽可笑。他用故事和笑话主导了谈话，每隔一句话就提到他的爱尔兰人或我的犹太人。他试图和女服务员开玩笑，想让她对每一道开胃菜和每瓶酒都有最深刻的看法，这太过分了，以至于坐在他旁边的我脸红了，好像在说：“嘿，我只是这里的客人！我不一定赞同这个套路！“。
但道林逃脱了惩罚，因为无论他每句话违反了多少禁忌，里面从来没有任何恶意的迹象。他是一个机会均等的罪犯，他最喜欢的目标是他自己。例如，他喜欢谈论我对童话般的抽象的病态痴迷，就像某种希望不会“崩溃”的“多项式层次”--笑点是，他，这个头脑坚定的激光物理学家，需要了解这对他自己的研究意味着什么。
美国南部的量子计算界，更不用说Twitter和Facebook，甚至整个世界，如果没有这种无与伦比的、比生活更响亮的存在，将会更加贫穷。
伊恩·T·达勒姆(Ian T Durham)说：当我第一次看到马克·王尔德关于这件事的帖子时，我简直不敢相信。我真的以为这是个玩笑，因为乔恩有如此扭曲的幽默感(我真的会怀念这一点)。我还是不确定我能不能相信。但除了他的幽默感外，他是一个非常和善的人，总是支持人们。我们刚刚失去了一个传奇人物。
评论政策：所有评论在出现之前都会进行审核和审核。评论可以出于任何原因保持适度，但特别是针对自相残杀的攻击、对人群的仇恨或嘲讽和居高临下的语气。另外：链接到论文或文章的评论，实际上是挑战我对它的回应，都有被适度留下的严重风险，因为这样的评论对我的时间提出了要求，我再也满足不了了。如果你在这里阐述你自己的论点，而不是把工作外包给别人，你会有更好的机会得到我的回应。有时我会在审核队列中意外错过完全合理的评论，或者它们会被垃圾邮件过滤器捕获。如果你觉得你的评论可能是这样的，给我发一封电子邮件。
note_en:
Today I woke up to the sad and shocking news that Jon Dowling ( homepage /  Twitter /  Wikipedia)—physics professor at Louisiana State, guy who got the US government to invest in quantum computing back in the 90s, author of the popular book  Schrödinger’s Killer App: Race to Build the World’s First Quantum Computer, investigator of BosonSampling among many other topics, owner of a “QUBIT” license plate, and one of my main competitors in the field of quantum computing humor—has passed away at age 65, apparently due to complications from surgery.
 Three months ago, right before covid shut down the world, the  last travel I did was a seven-hour road trip from Austin to Baton Rouge, together with my postdoc  Andrea Rocchetto, to deliver something called the  Hearne Lecture at the Louisiana State physics department. My topic (unsurprisingly) was Google’s quantum supremacy experiment.
 I’d debated whether to cancel the trip, as flying already seemed too dangerous. Dowling was the one who said “why not just drive here with one of your postdocs?”—which turned into a memorable experience for me and Andrea, complete with a personal tour of  LIGO and a visit to a baby alligator hatchery. I had no inkling that it was the last time I’d ever see Jon Dowling, but am now super-glad that we made the visit.
 At the dinner after my talk, Dowling was exactly the same as every other time I’d seen him: loud, piss-drunk, obnoxious, and hilarious. He dominated the conversation with stories and jokes, referring in every other sentence either to his Irishness or my Jewishness. His efforts to banter with the waitress, to elicit her deepest opinions about each appetizer and bottle of wine, were so over-the-top that I, sitting next to him, blushed, as if to say, “hey, I’m just the visitor here! I don’t necessarily  endorse this routine!”
 But Dowling got away with it because, no matter how many taboos he violated per sentence, there was never any hint of malice in it. He was an equal-opportunity offender, with his favorite target being himself. He loved to talk, for example, about my pathological obsession with airy-fairy abstractions, like some kind of “polynomial hierarchy” that hopefully wouldn’t “collapse”—with the punchline being that he, the hardheaded laser physicist, then needed to learn what that meant for his own research.
 The quantum computing community of the southern US, not to mention of Twitter and Facebook, and indeed of the entire world, will be poorer without this inimitable, louder-than-life presence.
     Ian T Durham Says:						 When I first saw Mark Wilde’s post about this I didn’t believe it. I honestly thought it was a joke because Jon had such a twisted sense of humor (which I am really going to miss). I’m still not sure I can believe it. But beyond his sense of humor, he was a supremely nice person and always supportive of people. We just lost a legend.

title: 乔纳森·道林(Jonathan Dowling，1955-2020)

note:
这是一篇不同寻常的长篇文章，因为这个问题如此复杂和微妙，值得适当的耐心和关注。它包括我非常诚实的，个人的，有人会说是冒险的观点，关于高绩效职业生涯的多样性，包括科技创业；以及我担心，一些人将多样性“武器化”的决定正在适得其反，并对代表不足的少数群体造成伤害。
我在休斯敦北部一个非常“丰富多彩”的地区长大，那里的邻居和同学通常是拉丁裔(移民和第二代)、黑人、亚洲人和白人的混血儿；社会经济的总体范围在福利、蓝领和某种中产阶级之间徘徊。我的父母(墨西哥移民)在一辆皮卡上开始了一项出售鳄梨和西红柿的农产品生意，最终发展壮大，但后来不幸地倒闭了。当我寄出大学申请时，我和我的两个姐妹靠在室内跳蚤市场卖香水的单身母亲养活。我最终就读于德克萨斯大学和哈佛法学院。今天，我有三个健康的孩子，婚姻幸福，结婚十多年，并在一家精英律师事务所担任成功的法律执业/领导职位，我舒适的“1%”生活绝对不会糟糕。
但这不是你的经典“美国梦”、“暴发户”、“看看我靠自己克服的一切有多棒”的故事。有一个关键的转折，这个转折给了我一个独特的视角来看待社会经济不平等、多样性以及心理健康问题。尽管我的母亲最终陷入了挣扎和不稳定的境地，但实际上她是墨西哥最顶尖的技术大学蒙特雷理工大学(Tecnologico de蒙特雷)的顶尖计算机科学毕业生。基本上就是墨西哥的麻省理工学院。她的家族血统包括一位全国著名的墨西哥艺术家和一位被认为是他所在领域最顶尖的医生和医学院教授之一的生父(但已经疏远)。
我的母亲是如何从一个有着深厚家庭背景的麻省理工学院同等学历的毕业生成长为在跳蚤市场卖香水的，作为一名徘徊在医疗补助水平的贫困边缘的单身母亲？这不是我的自传，所以我把故事的这一部分简而言之：精神疾病。许多人没有意识到，成功与智力/分析能力一样重要，而产生前者的公式往往比后者所需的公式复杂/微妙得多。
我提到关于我母亲的这些细节是因为它们是历史因素，如果我诚实地说，它们让我不能为你描绘一个完美的“除了我的靴子什么都不能让我振作起来”的故事。我母亲对学术的重视和对计算机的热爱-她尽一切努力让我在有限的能力范围内接触计算机-是我童年时期的关键战略资产，使我有别于我的同龄人，这无疑推动了我的进步。事实是，绝大多数给你讲这些“白手起家”故事的人，如果他们足够真诚，可以想出他们自己从小到大所依赖的各种特权。如果说有什么不同的话，那就是，仅仅是天生的“天赋”(用一个非常宽泛但经常使用的术语)本身就是一种不劳而获的特权，专属于少数幸运的人，他们往往喜欢方便地忽视自己的运气。
是的，我们是生活在低收入地区和破碎家庭的贫穷拉美人，说着西班牙语和英语的混合语言，吃着我们相当一部分的玉米粉蒸肉，薯条，芭芭卡等等。…。但是因为和学校有关，我的“家文化”就不一样了。我学习了。硬的。我的纪律水平让我(被其他拉丁人)多次被贴上“表现白人”的标签。甚至有一个专门的术语来形容它：“椰子”。外面是棕色的，里面是白色的。
我的拉丁裔朋友中很少有人在成长过程中成为成功的医生、律师、工程师、高管或企业家。他们中的许多人甚至没有上过大学。每当我听到有人提出关于拉美裔在美国“精英”经济阶层中代表性不足的统计数据时，我就会想起我童年的经历。我想我和我的朋友们对世界的看法是多么的不同，因为我在家里的期望，我的“椰子”期望。
我想，我经常听到，有时仍然会听到，成绩不佳的同龄人猛烈抨击世界对拉丁裔的种族主义和偏见；然而，有了像何塞这样的名字，我自己的种族并没有被“隐藏”。如果我生活的世界对拉丁裔有深刻的、系统性的歧视，那么我肯定是一个容易成为目标的人。然而我却在这里。当然，我遇到过一些真正的种族主义者，但他们很少能在有影响力的位置上改变我的轨迹。当然，还有一些刻板印象是我必须克服的。但是，再说一次，考虑到我遇到的有影响力的人和机构的进步性和功绩驱动型的性质(而且比
我已经学会了不被软弱的刻板印象冒犯，因为考虑到我成长的经历，我完全理解它们为什么会存在。在某种程度上，刻板印象是人类行为的一种正常功能，即使常常是不幸的。在我们家长大的过程中，我们有很多刻板印象的笑话，特别是关于白人的。仅仅是刻板印象并不能真正让你成为种族主义者。他们让你变得有人情味。决定你是否配得上这个标签的是你对一种刻板印象的坚持程度，以及你愿意给予个人怀疑的意愿。想方设法回击混蛋。但首先，不要急于(对你来说)毫无成效，不要对外面有多少人真的是混蛋得出错误的结论。
但让我们回到上述不幸的事实上：我的拉丁裔朋友中很少有成功的。为什么？种族主义？系统性歧视？我很抱歉，但你不能让我相信这一点。我承认刻板印象是存在的，它们确实在边缘影响了拉丁裔，但我自己的经历、观察和反思让我不可能接受今天拉美裔在高绩效行业的代表性严重不足是他们受到严重歧视的结果。差距太大了，这些行业中的许多行业竞争太激烈，利润太高，对于关键参与者来说，不能忽视真正未开发的人才被留在场边。
如果你想为强烈的、系统性的歧视拉丁裔辩护，你必须解释一下，比如说，古巴人和墨西哥人在结果上的巨大差距。一个深思熟虑和诚实的拉丁裔可以很快解释这种差距。多亏了卡斯特罗，古巴出现了“人才外流”。墨西哥没有。每个大国都有不同的阶层，有自己的亚文化和态度，包括关于教育、工作、家庭建设和许多其他生活因素的亚文化和态度。“墨西哥人”当然拥有共同的文化，但墨西哥银行家或教授与墨西哥劳工或餐馆老板对待教育的态度截然不同。由于历史和地理原因，移居美国的古巴人选择了更多的价值观，帮助他们在一个受教育和资本主义驱动的社会中取得更高水平的成功，而价值观相似的墨西哥人选择“呆在家里”的比例更高，而更多的蓝领同龄人(对学者的态度要不那么积极)离开美国。
你会看到这个问题在最近的非洲裔美国黑人之间突然出现-比如尼日利亚人和加纳人-相对于有着漫长的美国历史的非裔美国人，包括奴隶制。尼日利亚和加纳裔美国人及其子女平均而言在经济上比其他美国黑人成功得多，包括在某些方面比普通美国白人更成功。但就像古巴人和墨西哥人一样，他们看起来也会一模一样。显然，这种系统性歧视的说法比一些人所说的要复杂得多。移民模式造成了来自多个非洲国家的“人才外流”，这些国家往往会为那些拥有产生更积极结果的价值观的人口中的成员挑选人才。
当然，这并不是假装差异移民的选择过程本身就解释了不同群体的全部结果。儒家思想在解释为什么某些“亚洲”文化痴迷于他们的孩子的学业成就方面走了很长一段路。中国总体上比墨西哥更关心学校的表现。塔木德的历史同样解释了犹太人取得的巨大成就。解释摩门教徒就有点复杂了。但总的信息是，几个世纪的不同历史产生了具有不同价值观的不同文化，这些文化在不同的环境中产生了不同的表现。你知道有多少华裔美国精英棒球运动员？文化很重要。很多。
那么，为什么精英职业生涯中的拉美裔美国人数量如此之少，这是不成比例的呢？要给出一个完整的答案，需要一天的谈话时间和几个小时的写作时间。在社会经济层面上，美国的拉美裔显然面临着障碍，比如更高的贫困率，糟糕的学校，狭窄的住房，以及处于通常为新移民保留的较低经济阶层的所有挑战。无论你谈论的是哪个少数群体，这些因素都会压低长期业绩。然而，数据不可避免地表明，即使在控制社会经济障碍的情况下，某些群体在长期经济结果上仍然显著优于其他群体。
忽视文化价值的现实就是懦弱地把头埋在沙子里，因此永远不会完全解决这个问题。美国拉丁裔文化(通常)大力推动短期经济收益，而不是那种需要通常所说的“延迟满足”的长期成功。如果你刚走出高中就找到了一份体面的工作，
高绩效的职业，如法律、工程和医学，主要面向那些依赖于长期、复合培训和成就的技能。比方说，与营销或销售不同，在这些领域，有天赋的人不可能在K-12考试中表现得那么好，但仍然可以迅速吸收必要的技能，以便在毕业后获得高表现。而如果你想成为一名工程师或医生，在几个月甚至几年的时间里，很难弥补极其薄弱的数学和科学培训。
上天知道许多大学都在尝试(并为此祝福它们)，但它们就是不能放弃魔杖，抹去糟糕的公共教育和家庭环境(文化)，这些教育和家庭环境(文化)在十多年的过程中未能灌输学术严谨性，包括在孩子最发育的几年里。想一想成为一名世界级小提琴家需要做些什么。他们中有多少人是上了大学才开始训练的？几乎没有。孩子如何度过他们的时间在很大程度上影响着他们成年后能够达到的表现水平，特别是在依赖于复合技能的职业中。
因此，这些职业轨迹的性质-提醒你，这一性质绝对与种族歧视无关-严重不利于任何没有以相对坚实的学术背景出现在大学里的人。这意味着，拉美裔美国人(以及其他亚文化，比如阿巴拉契亚的农村白人)占了不成比例的比例。要真正解决这个问题，需要对童年、育儿、家庭结构和文化认同进行冗长、坦率的讨论。称“体制”为种族主义者无济于事。是的，某些群体比系统中的其他群体做得更好，但这通常是因为他们更好地让孩子们做好准备，以满足系统的逻辑要求。
现在，对于经常访问这个博客的读者来说，更狭隘的说法是：为什么成功的拉丁裔科技企业家如此之少？我已经解释了答案的一部分。尽管我们庆祝马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)和比尔·盖茨(Bill Gates)等人从哈佛辍学创办科技公司，但值得强调的是，首先，他们仍然被哈佛录取，其次，他们肯定在进入哈佛之前，在某个地方学习了创办科技公司所需的技能。科技创业依赖于一种复合技能，同样，这种技能会过滤掉那些童年时代未能帮助他们发展这一技能的人。扎克和比尔在大一的时候没有学过编程。
但是，让我们暂时把复合/技术技能问题放在一边，因为这里有一个更微妙的问题，这与我在讨论我母亲的糟糕结果时提出的观点有关，尽管她有明确的学术智力：成功取决于技术技能，但也取决于情感/心理韧性，特别是在某些环境中。
有一句经常被引用的本杰明·富兰克林(Benjamin Franklin)和约翰·亚当斯(John Adams)的话说，我完全会搞砸(并有点现代化)，但套用一下：人们成为农民，这样他们的孩子就可以成为商人，这样他们的孩子就可以成为专业人士，这样他们的孩子就可以成为艺术家和企业家。
这句话告诉我们什么？职业轨迹往往会有不同代际的进步，这种进步与经济稳定息息相关，而经济稳定本身又与心理韧性和冒险意愿息息相关。我可以告诉你，在与数百家创业企业合作并观察了数百家创业企业后，你的普通科技创业者并不是来自低收入，甚至不是蓝领/中产阶级背景。扎克和比尔当然没有。
通常需要一个童年环境，向一个人灌输对其家庭/网络的整体经济稳定性的信心，才能使他自信地承担成为企业家的巨大个人风险；而不是进入一个收入丰厚的职业。富有的孩子长大后成为企业家的可能性要大得多，因为他们知道，“破产”并不意味着实际上无家可归，相反，那里有大量的资源可以充当降落伞。具有类似技术或“智力”能力的孩子，但家庭生活在贫困的边缘，或者至少没有多少多余的资源来应对崩溃，就没有这种奢侈品了。
这并不是说拥有富裕背景的科技企业家不是大冒险家。他们大多数同样富有的朋友可能还在从事咨询和其他受薪工作。但至少要承认，拥有富裕的背景会极大地补贴个人的风险承受能力。
我一刻也不后悔，没有成为一名企业家，而是成为了一名VC律师。我的父母都没有任何实质性的积蓄。事实上，我支持我的母亲退休，而我的其余大部分家庭成员每天都在为留在中产阶级(定义宽松)而苦苦挣扎。与扎克或比尔不同的是，在我的情况下，失败确实会冒着无家可归的风险。我遇到过一些创业者，他们在创业时确实冒着无家可归/贫困的风险，但他们比人们经常在媒体上听到的英雄故事“不惜一切风险”中所暗示的少数人要少得多。
因此，我开始了一份对我的低收入家庭背景/历史有意义的职业。无怨无悔。我可以向你保证，无数有成就的拉丁裔(和其他少数民族)每天都会做出这个确切的决定。创业？抱歉，我还有父母和其他大家庭要帮忙。我要接受那份我辛辛苦苦牺牲的高薪工作。也许我的孩子会有更好的降落伞。
许多成就卓越的少数民族根本没有奢侈的父母、姑姑、叔叔、堂兄弟姐妹，他们都有稳定的收入和储蓄，为他们的创业冒险提供了降落伞。现在，他们的孩子可能会(参见约翰·亚当斯(John Adams)的路线)，但这就是为什么改变任何广泛群体的长期经济成功根本不可能成为现实的原因。最具挑战性的成功形式需要整个童年的复合学习，更进一步，最高风险的道路往往需要一代又一代人的财富和韧性的建立。
因此，首先，最新的复合技术技能“过滤器”对拉丁裔(和类似群体)的影响不成比例，因为文化因素，而不仅仅是社会经济因素，导致他们中较少的人在童年时期发展复合技能，从而使科技创业成为可能。其次，对于最终仍能做到这一点的不成比例的少数人来说，他们来自稳定的经济背景的可能性远远低于其他群体，而稳定的经济背景使得创业等高风险道路具有吸引力。现在，让我们问自己一个棘手的问题。在任何短期意义上，招聘人员和投资者实际上能对这种不幸的现实产生多大的实质性影响？
低收入移民的后代需要时间来建立那种鼓励非常高风险、非常高回报道路的经济稳定性和韧性。任何希望真正影响长期集体结果的人都需要面对、正面面对，而不是无益的愤怒或相互指责，帮助人们满足高绩效服务的要求需要时间这一事实。它还需要诚实地对待文化/行为价值观，这些价值观让孩子们长大后才能面对这些要求。但是，这些要求本身，无论我们多么希望如此，都不会动摇。
我们喜欢认为创业，包括科技创业，是一个非常民主化的，平等的竞争环境，任何有正确动机和毅力的人都可以成功；但我诚实的经验是，今天，实际上，情况正好相反。成功的创业需要非常罕见和复杂的组合，包括分析和技术技能、沟通技能、社交技能和对当前和不断变化的市场状况的创造性判断，此外还有令人难以置信的心理韧性。对于任何想玩的人来说，现代高度竞争的创业精神并不是一个容易进入的领域，而是代表着人类能力、创造力和耐力的巅峰。我们不应该感到惊讶的是，对于大多数人来说，达到这一顶峰的必要条件(但不是充分条件)早在成年之前就开始了；而且还包括支持高成就的童年环境。
把所有这些联系在一起：我希望看到更多的“有色人种”在科技创业和其他高绩效的职业生涯中，就像所有其他善良、诚实、进步的人一样。但我担心，社区中的一部分-无论是善意的还是非善意的-已经选择将多样性问题武器化，这种方式不仅充满敌意和不真诚，而且对自己的事业适得其反。
这是有历史、文化和社会经济原因的。

note_en:
This is an unusually lengthy essay, because the issue is so complex and nuanced that it deserves an appropriate level of patience and attention. It includes my deeply honest, personal, and some would say risky perspective on the topic of diversity in high-performance careers, including tech entrepreneurship; and my concern that the decision by some to “weaponize” diversity is backfiring and causing harm to under-represented minority groups.
 I grew up in a very “colorful” part of Northside Houston, with neighbors and schoolmates who were usually a mix of latino (immigrants and 2nd generation), black, asian and white; and a general socioeconomic range hovering between welfare, blue collar, and sort-of-middle class. My parents (Mexican immigrants) started a produce business selling avocados and tomatoes out of a pickup truck, that eventually grew but then unfortunately imploded. By the time I was sending off college applications, my two sisters and I were supported by our single mother who sold perfumes at an indoor flea market. I ended up attending the University of Texas and Harvard Law School. Today, with three healthy kids, happily married over a decade and a successful legal practice/leadership position at an elite boutique law firm, my cozy “1%” life definitely does  not suck.
 But this isn’t your classic “American Dream” “Rags to Riches” “look at how awesome I am for everything I’ve overcome  on my own” story. There’s a key twist, and that twist has given me a unique perspective on issues of socioeconomic inequality, diversity, and also mental health. My mother, despite ending up in a struggling, unstable situation, was actually a top computer science graduate of the most elite technology university in Mexico; the Tecnologico de Monterrey. Basically the MIT of Mexico. And  her family pedigree includes a nationally celebrated Mexican artist and a biological (but estranged) father considered one of the top Medical doctors and Medical School professors in his field.
 How did my mom go from an MIT-equivalent grad with a strong family background to selling perfumes at a flea market as a single mom hovering preciously close to Medicaid-level poverty? This isn’t my autobiography, so I’ll cut that part of the story short and summarize: mental illness. Many people fail to appreciate how success is just as much about emotional intelligence/stability as it is about intellectual/analytical capacity, and the formula for producing the former is often far more complex/nuanced than what’s necessary for the latter.
 I mentioned these details about my mother because they are historical elements that keep me, if I’m honest, from painting for you a perfect “picked myself up by nothing but my bootstraps” story. My mother’s emphasis on academics and her love of computers – which she made every effort to expose me to, within her limited means – were key strategic assets in my childhood that differentiated me from my peer group, and undoubtedly propelled me forward. The truth is the  vast majority of people who give you these “rags to riches” stories can, if they’re sincere enough, come up with their own kinds of privilege that they depended on growing up. If anything, simply being born “gifted” (to use a very broad but frequently used term) is itself an unearned privilege reserved for a lucky few who often like to conveniently overlook their luck.
 Yes, we were poor latinos living in a low-income area and a broken home, speaking a blend of spanish and english and having our fair share of tamales, frijoles, barbacoa, etc. etc… but as it related to school, my “home culture” was different. I studied. Hard. With a level of discipline that got me labeled as “acting white” (by other latinos) more than enough times. There was even a special term for it: “coconut.” Brown on the outside, white on the inside.
 Very very few of my latino friends growing up have ended up as successful doctors, lawyers, engineers, executives, or entrepreneurs. Many of them didn’t even go to college. Whenever I hear someone bring up statistics about the under-representation of latinos among the “elite” economic strata in the U.S., I think about my childhood experiences. I think about how differently I saw the world from my friends, because of the expectations I had at home; my “coconut” expectations.
 I think about how often I heard, and sometimes still hear, underachieving peers lash out at the world for its racism and prejudice against latinos; and yet, with a name like Jose, there was no “hiding” my own ethnicity. If the world I was living in had deep,  systemic discrimination against latinos, surely I was an easy target. And yet here I am. Certainly I’ve encountered some bona fide racists, but rarely were they in positions of influence to alter my trajectory. And of course there are stereotypes that I’ve had to overcome. But, again, given the progressiveness and merit-driven nature of the people and institutions of influence I’ve encountered (and thank God for that), the stereotypes eventually gave way to what I could  actually deliver.
 I’ve learned to not be so offended by weak stereotypes, because given my experiences growing up, I understand full well why they exist. Stereotyping is at some level a normal, even if often unfortunate, function of human behavior. We had plenty of stereotype jokes in our household growing up, particularly about white people. Stereotypes alone do not really make you a racist. They make you human. It’s how strongly you hold onto a stereotype, and your willingness to give individuals the benefit of the doubt, that determines whether or not you deserve the label. By all means push back hard against assholes. But first, don’t jump to unproductive (for you) and incorrect conclusions about how many people out there really are assholes.
 But let’s jump back to the unfortunate above-mentioned fact: very few of my latino friends ended up successful. Why? Racism? Systemic discrimination? I’m sorry, but you won’t convince me of that. I will acknowledge that stereotypes are out there, and they do impact latinos at the margins, but my own experiences, observations, and reflections make it impossible for me to accept that the  dramatic under-representation of latinos in high-performance industries  today is the result of them being  discriminated against. The gap is simply too large, and many of these industries are simply too competitive and lucrative for key players to ignore truly untapped talent being kept on the sidelines.
 If you want to argue for strong, systemic discrimination against latinos, you’ll have to explain the very big gaps in outcomes between, say, Cubans and Mexicans. A reflective and honest latino can explain that gap very quickly. Cuba had a “brain drain” thanks to Castro. Mexico did not. Every major country has different strata with their own subcultures and attitudes, including about education, work, family building, and many other life factors. “Mexicans” certainly share a common culture, but a Mexican banker or professor has a  very different attitude toward education than a Mexican laborer or restaurant owner. History and geography have caused Cuban immigration to the United States to  select more for Cubans with values that help them succeed at higher levels of performance in an education and capitalism driven society, while Mexicans with similar values have disproportionately chosen to “stay home,” with their much more blue collar counterparts (with a far less positive attitude toward academics) leaving to America.
 You see this issue pop up between black Americans of recent African descent – like Nigerians and Ghanaians – relative to African-Americans with lengthy American histories, including with slavery. Nigerian and Ghanaian Americans, and their children, are  far more economically successful on average than other black Americans, including by some measures more successful than average white Americans. But just like Cubans and Mexicans, they  look exactly the same. Clearly this  systemic discrimination narrative is more complex than some make it out to be. Immigration patterns have generated a “brain drain” from various African countries that  selects for members of those populations with values that generate more positive outcomes.
 Of course, this isn’t to pretend that the selection process of differential migration  alone explains the full outcomes of different groups. Confucianism goes a  long way in explaining why certain “Asian” cultures are obsessive about academic achievement for their children. China  broadly cares a whole lot more about school performance than Mexico does. Talmudic history similarly explains the strong outcomes of Jews. Explaining Mormons is a little more complicated. But the general message is centuries of distinct history produce distinct cultures with distinct values that generate distinct kinds of performance in distinct environments. How many elite Chinese-American baseball players are you aware of? Culture matters. A lot.
 So why  is there such a disproportionately small number of American latinos in elite careers? To give a full answer, it would take a day’s worth of conversations, and hours of writing. On a socioeconomic level, latinos in America obviously face barriers like higher amounts of poverty, poor schools, cramped housing, and all the challenges of being in the lower economic brackets typically reserved for recent immigrants. Regardless of what minority group you are talking about, these factors push down long-term performance. Yet the data unavoidably shows that, even when controlling for socioeconomic barriers, certain groups still dramatically outperform others on long-term economic outcomes.
 To ignore the reality of cultural values is to cowardly stick your head in the sand, and therefore never fully address the issue. American Latino culture (generally) heavily promotes short-term economic gain over the kind of long-term success requiring what’s often called “delayed gratification.” If you find a decent job right outside of high school, and even during high school, that can afford you a relatively nice car and possibly a modest home, your “network” as a latino is far more likely to celebrate your achievement. If you grind out your high school years acing AP classes, forgoing short-term economic opportunities, in hopes of getting a BA and even a masters degree, you’re more likely to be called a coconut.
 American Latino culture (specifically the culture of the strata of latinos  who come to America, which is very different from “Latin American” culture generally), for all of the above-covered reasons having to do with history and immigration selection, disproportionately prioritizes blue collar and middle-class success over the long-term delayed training required to reach the upper tiers of high-performance professions. Where are the millions of Mexican parents who demand academic excellence from their kids? Largely in Mexico, where they are successful and have no reason to leave.
 High-performance professions, like law, engineering, and medicine, are heavily oriented toward the kinds of skills that depend on long-term, compounding training and achievement. Unlike, say, marketing or sales, where someone with a natural gift could  possibly not do that well in K-12 and still quickly absorb necessary skills for high-performance post-school, it is  very hard to make up, in a matter of a few months or even years, for extremely weak math and science training if you want to be an engineer or doctor.
 Heaven knows many universities try (and bless them for it), but they simply cannot waive a magic wand and erase a poor public education and home environment (culture) that failed to instill academic rigor over the course of over a decade, including during a child’s most formative years. Think of what it takes to be a world class violinist. How many of them started training only when they got to university? Virtually none. How a child spends their time heavily influences the performance level they are able attain as an adult, especially in careers dependent on compounding skillsets.
 Thus, these career tracks by their nature – a nature that has absolutely nothing to do with racial discrimination, mind you – heavily disfavor anyone who doesn’t show up to college with a relatively solid academic background. That means, disproportionately, American latinos (and also other subcultures, like, say, rural whites in Appalachia). To really address this problem requires a lengthy, candid discussion about childhood, parenting, family structure, and cultural identity. Calling “the system” racist gets you nowhere. Yes, certain groups do better than others in the system, but that’s generally because they better prepare children to meet the logical requirements of the system.
 Now speaking even more narrowly for the audience that frequents this blog: why are there so few successful latino tech entrepreneurs? I’ve already explained  part of the answer. As much as we celebrate people like Mark Zuckerberg and Bill Gates dropping out of Harvard to start tech companies, it’s worth emphasizing that  first they still got into Harvard, and  second they surely must’ve learned the skills necessary to start  tech companies somewhere before getting to Harvard. Tech entrepreneurship is dependent on a compounding skillset that, again, filters out people whose childhoods failed to help them develop that skillset. Zuck and Bill didn’t learn to code during their freshman years.
 But let’s put aside the compounding/technical skill issue for a moment, because there’s a more nuanced issue at play here, and it relates to the point I made when discussing my mother’s poor outcome despite her clear academic intelligence: success depends on  technical skill but also, particularly in certain environments, on  emotional/psychological resilience.
 There’s a frequently cited  Benjamin Franklin John Adams line that I am totally going to botch (and somewhat modernize), but to paraphrase: people become farmers, so that  their children can become merchants, so that  their children can become professionals, so that  their children can become artists and entrepreneurs.
 What is this line telling us? That there is often a  generational progression to career trajectories, and that progression is tied to economic stability, which itself is tied to psychological resilience and willingness to take risk. I can tell you, having worked with and observed hundreds of entrepreneurial ventures, your  average tech entrepreneur is not coming from a low-income or even blue collar/middle class background. Zuck and Bill certainly did not.
 It often takes a childhood environment that infuses a person with confidence about their family/network’s overall economic stability to enable that person to confidently take the enormous personal risk of becoming an entrepreneur; instead of going into a nicely salaried career. Wealthy kids are  far more likely to grow up to become entrepreneurs, because they know that going “bust” doesn’t mean actually going homeless, but instead there’s an enormous set of resources there to serve as a parachute.  Kids with similar technical or “intellectual” ability, but with families living on the precipice of poverty, or at least with very few excess resources to manage a blowup, do not have that luxury.
 This is  not to say that tech entrepreneurs with wealthy backgrounds are not big risk takers. Most of their equally wealthy friends probably still went into consulting and other salaried jobs. But it is to at least  acknowledge that having an affluent background dramatically subsidizes personal risk tolerance.
 I do not regret  for a second becoming a VC lawyer instead of becoming an entrepreneur. Neither of my parents had any substantial savings. In fact, I support my mother in her retirement, and most of the rest of my family struggles on a daily basis to even stay in the middle class (loosely defined). Unlike Zuck or Bill, failure in my case would’ve, quite literally, risked homelessness. I have met entrepreneurs who really did risk homelessness/poverty in starting their entrepreneurial ventures, but they are a far smaller minority than is suggested from the heroic “risk absolutely everything” stories one often hears in the media.
 So I went into a career that made sense for  my low-income family background/history. No regrets. I can guarantee you countless high-achieving latinos (and other minorities) make this exact decision on a daily basis. Startup? Sorry, I’ve got parents and other extended family to help out. I’ll take that high-salaried job I busted my ass and sacrificed for. Maybe my kids will have a better parachute.
 Many high-achieving minorities simply do not have the luxury of parents, aunts, uncles, cousins all with stable incomes and savings to provide them a parachute for an entrepreneurial adventure. Now,  their kids might (see the John Adams line), but that’s why changing the long-term economic success of any broad group simply cannot be wished into existence. The most challenging forms of success take an entire childhood of compounding learning, and going even further, the very highest risk paths often take  generational building of wealth and resilience.
 So, first, the  compounding technical skill “filter” disproportionately impacts latinos (and similar groups) because cultural, not just socioeconomic, factors lead fewer of them to develop the compounding skills in childhood that make tech entrepreneurship feasible. Second, for the disproportionately small number of them who still end up getting there, they are  far less likely than other groups to come from the kinds of stable economic backgrounds that make high-risk paths like entrepreneurship attractive. Now, let’s ask ourselves a hard question. How much of this unfortunate reality can  actually be materially impacted by recruiters and investors in any short-term sense?
 It takes  time for the descendants of low-income immigrants to build the kind of economic stability and resilience that encourages very high-risk, very high-reward paths. Anyone expecting to  actually impact long-term collective outcomes needs to face, head on and without unproductive outrage or finger-pointing, the fact that helping people meet the requirements of high-performance takes time. It also takes honesty about the cultural/behavioral values that build up children to face those requirements as adults. The requirements themselves, no matter how much we might wish otherwise, are not going to budge.
 We like to think of entrepreneurship, including tech entrepreneurship, as a very democratizing, equalizing playing field where anyone with the right motivation and persistence can succeed; but my honest experience is that today, in fact, it’s the opposite. High-achieving entrepreneurship takes a spectacularly rare and complex combination of analytical and technical skills, communication skills, social skills, and creative judgment about current and changing market conditions, on top of incredible psychological resilience. Rather than an easily accessible field for anyone who wants to play, modern hyper-competitive entrepreneurship represents the apex of human abilities, creativity, and endurance. We should not be surprised that, for most people, the necessary (but not sufficient) conditions for reaching that apex start well before adulthood; and include a childhood environment that supports high achievement.
 Tying this all together: I want to see more “people of color” in tech entrepreneurship and other high-performance careers just like all other good, honest, progressive people. But I’m afraid that a segment of the community – either well-intentioned or not – has chosen to  weaponize the issue of diversity in a way that is not only hostile and disingenuous, but counterproductive to its own cause.

note:
作为长期支持计划的一部分，Blender 2.83 LTS提供了主要项目所需的性能和稳定性，已修复了超过1250个错误，并将在未来两年内进行进一步的关键修复。新功能包括VR支持、OpenVDB导入、OptiX视口去噪和强大的新物理布刷。
现在可以使用新的Volume对象导入和渲染OpenVDB文件。文件可以由Blender的流体模拟缓存或甚至其他软件生成。
在OpenXR的支持下，Blender现在附带了VR支持的第一个里程碑：场景检查。在搅拌机中直接在虚拟现实中漫游您的场景！
循环现在支持使用NVIDIA OptiX™AI加速消噪器在搅拌器视口内以及在最终渲染中进行消噪。以完全视觉保真度预览渲染场景，无噪波。
在你的画笔下进行物理模拟。使用新的布刷雕刻逼真的褶皱。其内置的物理解算器自动创建看起来自然的折叠。
模拟的质量和阻尼属性是笔刷的设置。另外两个控制器限制模拟的影响和衰减。
在模拟过程中，被遮罩的顶点充当固定顶点，重力直接应用于解算器中。
网格过滤器中的新锐化模式在自动平滑平面的同时捏捏边缘。
这种新的笔刷(也可用作网格过滤器)使用HC算法从“噪声曲面网格的改进拉普拉斯平滑”中删除了曲面，同时保留了对象的体积。通过对原始形状保持、位移和迭代次数的调整，可以控制平滑效果。
粘土笔刷也得到了改进，并增加了一个新的选项，可以在圆形和正方形之间选择笔刷笔尖。
图层笔刷被完全重新设计。在相加和相减之间交替或多次修改同一区域时，伪影消失了。它的特点是在光标中预览图层高度，对永久基础的多分辨率支持，以及更好的掩码支持。
在工作时保持复杂网格的有序组织。新的面集系统允许您控制网格各个部分的可见性，或自动遮罩部分曲面以进行雕刻。
基于编辑模式选择、松散网格孤岛、材质、法线、接缝等创建面集。
姿势笔刷可以将旋转原点捕捉到面集的边界。
从头开始重写，油脂铅笔2D动画工具集现在速度更快-并且更好地与Blender的其余部分集成。
您所知道的网格工作流程现在可用于油脂铅笔对象！使笔触的颜色不再局限于单一的材料，现在每个点都有自己的颜色，给你无限的艺术自由。
新的系统允许你组合面具，增加了很大的灵活性，多亏了新的绘图引擎，它看起来也更好了。
重写了效果修改器以提高质量和速度，同时添加了新功能以使其更加灵活。
现在，在处理具有大量油脂铅笔笔划的文件时，帧速率提高了2倍，并且在快速绘制时笔划更加平滑。
波浪纹理节点获得了新的方向模式、相位偏移控制和粗糙度输入，并且改进了白噪波纹理、数学和向量数学节点。多读点。
新的角度滑块使您可以轻松旋转矢量。试试这个示例.blend文件！
在雕刻模式下，新的延迟视口更新选项可提高导航高多边形网格时的性能。
Blender的视频编辑器继续看到其用户界面的改进，这是一个改善用户体验和性能的工具栏。
需要使用函数blender_srgb_to_frame buffer_space修补自定义着色器。这可确保将输出颜色转换到正确的颜色空间。(提交、任务)
支持每个人维护和改进搅拌机的捐赠计划。每月只需支付€5美元即可成为会员。
note_en:
With over 1250 bugfixes, and further critical fixes due throughout the next two years as part of the Long Term Support program, Blender 2.83 LTS provides the performance and stability needed for major projects. New features include VR support, OpenVDB import, OptiX viewport denoising and a powerful new physics-enabled Cloth Brush.
 OpenVDB files can now be imported and rendered with the  new Volume object. Files may be generated by Blender’s fluid simulation cache or even other software.
 Powered by  OpenXR, Blender now ships with the first milestone in VR support:  scene inspection. Walk around your scenes in virtual reality directly within Blender!
 Cycles now supports denoising inside the Blender viewport with   NVIDIA OptiX™ AI-Accelerated Denoiser  as well as in final renders. Preview your rendered scene at full visual fidelity, without noise.
 Physics simulation under your brush. Sculpt realistic wrinkles with the new Cloth Brush. Its built-in physics solver automatically creates natural-looking folds.
 The  mass and the  damping properties of the simulation are settings of the brush. Two additional controllers limit the influence and falloff of the simulation.
 Masked vertices act as pinned during the simulation, and the gravity is applied directly in the solver.
   The new Sharpen mode in Mesh Filter pinches the edges while smoothing flat surfaces automatically.
  This new brush (also available as Mesh Filter) removes the surface while preserving the volume of the object usin the HC algorithm from “Improved Laplacian Smoothing of Noisy Surface Meshes”. The smooth result an be controlled by tweaing the original shape preservation, displacement and iteration count.
  The Clay brush also got improvements and a new option to choose the brush tip between circle and square.
  The Layer Brush was completely redesigned. Gone are the artifacts when alternating between adding and subtracting or when modifying the same area multiple times. It features a preview of the Layer height in the cursor, Multires support for Persistent Base and much better masking support.
  Keep complex meshes organized as you work. The new Face Sets system lets you control the visibility of individual parts of a mesh, or automatically mask parts of its surface for sculpting.
 Create Face Sets based on edit mode selection, loose mesh islands, materials, normals, seams, and more.
    The Pose Brush can snap the rotation origin points to the boundaries of the Face Sets.
 Rewritten from scratch, the Grease Pencil 2D animation toolset is now  much faster – and much better integrated with the rest of Blender.
  The same workflow you know for meshes is now available for Grease Pencil objects!  Making stroke colors no longer limited to a single material, now each point has its own color giving you unlimited artistic freedom.
   The new system allows you to combine masks, adding a lot of flexibility and it also looks much better thanks to the new draw-engine.
  The effect modifiers have been rewritten to improve quality and speed, while adding new functions to make them more flexible.
  Frame rates are now 2x higher when working on files with large numbers of Grease Pencil strokes, and strokes are smoother when painting quickly.
  The Wave Texture node gets new direction modes, a phase offset control and a roughness input, and the White Noise Texture, Math and Vector Math nodes have been improved.  Read more.
 The new Angle slider lets you easily rotate the vectors.   Try this example .blend file!
 In Sculpt mode, the new  Delay Viewport Updates option improves performance when navigating high-poly meshes
  Blender’s video editor continues to see improvements in its user interface, a toolbar to improve user experience and performance.
       Custom shaders need to be patched using the function blender_srgb_to_framebuffer_space. This ensure the output color is transformed to the correct color space. ( commit,  task)
   The donation program to support maintaining and improving Blender, for everyone.									 Become a member for as little as €5 per month.

1883年2月，南太平洋铁路为想要向西进军的美国人开辟了一条新的道路。它的横贯大陆的“日落路线”正式建成，直接连接新奥尔良和加利福尼亚州。这条创新的运输线是连接东南和太平洋的第二条横贯大陆的线路，不仅给狂野的西部带来了新的商业，而且改变了移民模式，改变了相对较新的加利福尼亚州。特别值得一提的是，洛杉矶今天的黑人人口受到克里奥尔人的影响，这是日落路线的直接结果。
负责这一雄心勃勃的遍及全国的四位铁路大亨是科利斯·P·亨廷顿、查尔斯·克罗克、利兰·斯坦福和马克·霍普金斯。竞争激烈的亨廷顿试图利用他的公司对加州铁轨85%的主导地位，铺设一条穿越南方的铁路。亨廷顿通过自己的资金和其他三家公司的资金(也是有针对性的贿赂)，控制了几条较小的铁路，从而诞生了日落路线。(应该指出的是，多亏了中国移民的低薪和危险劳动，它才得以建成和连接。)。
这条路线覆盖了惊人的9000英里长的轨道，这是适度的近似。就这样，“四大”在太平洋沿岸实现了对航运业的牵制，确保了他们在那里港口的利益得到重视。作为一个额外的好处，这条铁路推动了洛杉矶的商业货运，这助长了随后的“投机土地泡沫”，这一泡沫在本世纪末起飞。
圣何塞州立大学(San Jose State University)社会学家福斯蒂娜·杜克罗斯(Faustina Ducros)表示，虽然“加州经历了几个阶段的经济繁荣”，但日落路线给该州带来的财务收益对该州的经济尤为关键。她说：“通往西部的铁路线从建设之日起就是其发展不可或缺的一部分。”举个恰当的例子：四大巨头可能会收取高额运费，以收回他们可观的投资。对于一个历史上主要交通形式是牛车和马车的城市来说，日落路线第一次帮助洛杉矶向全球经济开放。
由于加州当时闪闪发光的经济可能性，该州开始吸引来自全国其他地区的人。具体地说，来自南方的美国黑人在19世纪和20世纪初开始大量移民到洛杉矶。“许多搬到西部的南方黑人是为了逃避吉姆·克罗种族隔离带来的种族不平等，”杜克罗斯说。“(他们)在为家人寻找更好的工作和教育机会。”
这条直达加利福尼亚州的路线-天气晴朗，全年都有-让在那里开始生活相对来说没有痛苦，但实际上，任何事情都比另一种选择更好。今天，加利福尼亚州的南方黑人及其后代的人数反映了摆脱奴隶制和种族隔离阴影的共同利益。杜克罗斯说：“从1940年到1950年，洛杉矶黑人人口的增长翻了一番，许多新居民都是通过铁路线来的。”这些移民中的许多人都是通过日落路线旅行的，他们在靠近火车站和工作岗位的市中心定居下来。虽然这些南方人中的一些人正在寻求新的生活方式，并计划在他们到达后找到工作，但他们中的一些人是通过他们的工作来到加州的。Ducros指出：“普尔曼搬运工受雇于铁路，他们经常是西迁家庭中的大移民先锋，因为他们在他们的路线上看到了加州。”
今天，洛杉矶仍然反映着沿着铁路带到加州的南方影响。Ducros说：“即使是现在，洛杉矶南部曾经熙熙攘攘的路易斯安那州社区也留下了遗迹。”“你仍然可以看到餐馆和企业迎合路易斯安那州的口味。南洛杉矶的一些天主教教区有大量来自路易斯安那州的教区居民，聚居区至今仍保留着。“。
南太平洋铁路的日落线路现在被称为美铁的“日落有限公司”列车(使其成为美国最古老的命名线路)，自2005年卡特里娜飓风冲毁了部分铁路线以来，该线路已部分停运。但它对南加州的影响更持久。
note_en:
In February 1883, the Southern Pacific Railroad created a new path for Americans who wanted to head out West. Its transcontinental “Sunset Route” was officially completed, connecting New Orleans directly to California. This innovative transit line, the second transcontinental connection, between the Southeast and the Pacific not only brought new commerce to the wild, wild West, but shifted migratory patterns in a way that changed the relatively new state of California. In particular, Los Angeles’s present-day black population has creole influences that are a direct result of the Sunset Route.
 The four railroad barons responsible for this ambitious reach across the nation were Collis P. Huntington, Charles Crocker, Leland Stanford, and Mark Hopkins. The ever-competitive Huntington sought to capitalize on his company’s  85 percent dominance over California’s tracks by laying down a railway through the South. Through his own capital and funds from the other three (targeted bribery, too), Huntington gained control of several smaller railroads, giving birth to the Sunset Route. (It should be noted that it was completed and connected thanks to the low-paid and dangerous labor of Chinese immigrants.)
 The route covered a whopping 9,000 miles of track, by modest approximation. And just like that, the “Big Four” had achieved a stranglehold over the shipping industry on the Pacific seaboard, which ensured that their interests in the ports there would be taken seriously. As an added bonus, this railroad pushed commercial freight through Los Angeles, which fueled the subsequent “ speculative land bubble” that took off later in the decade.
  Faustina DuCros, a sociologist at San Jose State University, says that while “California has seen several phases of economic boom,” the financial gains the Sunset Route brought to the state were particularly pivotal to its economy. “The rail lines to the West were integral to its development ever since they were being constructed,” she says. Case in point: The four barons could charge high shipping rates to make back their considerable investment. For a city whose primary form of transit had historically been ox-carts and wagons, the Sunset Route helped open Los Angeles up to global economy for the first time.
 As a result of the economic possibilities California was then glittering with, the state began to attract people from other regions of the country. Specifically, black Americans from the South began migrating in large numbers to Los Angeles in the 19th and early 20th centuries. “Many of the black Southerners moving West were escaping racial inequality that came out of Jim Crow segregation,” DuCros says. “[They were] looking for better work and educational opportunities for their family.”
 This straight shot to California—fair-weather, year-round—made starting a life there relatively painless, but really, anything was better than the alternative. The number of black Southerners and their descendants in California today reflect this common interest in leaving the shadow of slavery and segregation behind. “The growth of the black population in Los Angeles doubled from 1940 to 1950, and many new residents made their way via the rail lines,” DuCros says. Many of these migrants, who had traveled via the Sunset Route, settled in the central area of the city, which was close to the rail station and jobs. While some of these Southerners were seeking a new lifestyle and planned to find work once they arrived, some of them arrived in California through their work. “The Pullman porters were employed on the railroads and were often the Great Migration pioneers in the families moving westward because they had seen California on their routes,” DuCros notes.
  Today, Los Angeles still reflects the Southern influences that were brought to California along the railroad. “Even now, there are remnants of what was once a bustling Louisiana community in South Los Angeles,” DuCros says. “You still see restaurants and businesses catering to Louisiana-inspired tastes. Some of the Catholic parishes in South Los Angeles had large population of parishioners from Louisiana, and clusters still remain to this day.”
 The Southern Pacific Railroad’s Sunset Route is now known as Amtrak’s “Sunset Limited” train (making it the oldest named route in the country), and has been partially suspended since 2005, when Hurricane Katrina washed out part of the rail line. But its impact on Southern California is more durable.
title: “日落之路”铁路如何帮助加州实现多元化
title_en: How the ‘Sunset Route’ Railroad Helped Diversify California

在过去五年里，密苏里州弗格森、巴尔的摩以及现在的明尼阿波利斯等城市发生警察暴力事件后，要求改革的呼声越来越高，警察工会已经成为改革的最重要障碍之一。要求改革的政治压力越大，工会抵制改革的力度就越大-几乎没有市政府官员，包括自由派领导人，能够克服他们的反对。
他们积极保护被控行为不当的成员的权利，通常是在仲裁听证会上，他们一直在努力闭门造车。他们还非常有效地抵御了更广泛的变化，利用他们的政治影响力和影响力来破坏加强问责的努力。
虽然自20世纪80年代初以来，全国的工会会员率下降了一半，降至10%，但警察工会中更高的会员率让他们有了资源，可以花在阻止改革的运动和诉讼上。自2014年以来，纽约市的一个警察工会已经在州和地方竞选上花费了100多万美元。
在圣路易斯，当四年前金·加德纳(Kim Gardner)被选为最高检察官时，她开始控制该市警察暴力的高比率。但在她提议在检察官办公室内成立一个独立调查不当行为的小组后，她遇到了强大的当地警察工会。
工会向立法者施压，要求他们搁置这项提案，许多人支持该提案，但随后从未进行投票。大约在同一时间，工会的一名律师发起了一场法律斗争，以限制检察官办公室调查警察不当行为的能力。第二年，工会的一位领导人说，加德纳应该“通过武力或选择”被免职。
长期以来，试图跨越警察工会的政客们一直担心被工会贴上犯罪软弱的标签，或者出现更严重的后果。
明尼阿波利斯市议员史蒂夫·弗莱彻(Steve Fletcher)经常批评警察局，他说，当他试图将资金从招聘警察转移到一个新成立的暴力预防办公室时，警方对他的选民拨打的911电话的反应变得不那么快了。“它的运作有点像保护费，”弗莱彻在谈到工会时说。
在明尼阿波利斯检察官指控一名警官谋杀乔治·弗洛伊德(George Floyd)的几天后，该市警察工会主席谴责了政治领导人，指责他们出卖了自己的成员，并在没有正当程序的情况下解雇了四名警官。
“这是卑鄙的行为，”工会主席鲍勃·克罗尔中尉(Lt.Bob Kroll)在一封写给工会成员的信中写道，当地记者获得了这封信。他还将抗议者称为“恐怖运动”。
克罗尔本人至少受到了29起投诉，他还指责奥巴马政府“压迫警察”，并称赞特朗普总统是“给罪犯戴上手铐的人，而不是给我们戴上手铐的人”。
note_en:
Over the past five years, as demands for reform have mounted in the aftermath of police violence in cities like Ferguson, Mo., Baltimore and now Minneapolis, police unions have emerged as one of the most significant roadblocks to change. The greater the political pressure for reform, the more defiant the unions often are in resisting it — with few city officials, including liberal leaders, able to overcome their opposition.
 They aggressively protect the rights of members accused of misconduct, often in arbitration hearings that they have battled to keep behind closed doors. And they have also been remarkably effective at fending off broader change, using their political clout and influence to derail efforts to increase accountability.
 While rates of union membership have dropped by half nationally since the early 1980s, to 10 percent,  higher membership rates among police unions give them resources they can spend on campaigns and litigation to block reform. A single New York City police union has spent more than $1 million on state and local races since 2014.
 In St. Louis, when Kim Gardner was elected the top prosecutor four years ago, she set out to rein in the city’s  high rate of police violence. But after she proposed a unit within the prosecutor’s office that would independently investigate misconduct, she ran into the powerful local police union.
 The union pressured lawmakers to set aside the proposal, which many supported but then never brought to a vote. Around the same time, a lawyer for the union waged a legal fight to limit the ability of the prosecutor’s office to investigate police misconduct. The following year, a leader of the union said Ms. Gardner should be removed “by force or by choice.”
 Politicians tempted to cross police unions have long feared being labeled soft on crime by the unions, or more serious consequences.
 When Steve Fletcher, a Minneapolis city councilman and frequent Police Department critic, sought to divert money away from hiring officers and toward a newly created office of violence prevention, he said, the police stopped responding as quickly to 911 calls placed by his constituents. “It operates a little bit like a protection racket,” Mr. Fletcher said of the union.
  A few days after prosecutors in Minneapolis charged an officer with murder in the death of George Floyd, the president of the city’s police union denounced political leaders, accusing them of selling out his members and firing four officers without due process.
 “It is despicable behavior,” the union president, Lt. Bob Kroll, wrote in a  letter to union members obtained by a local reporter. He also referred to protesters as a “terrorist movement.”
 Mr. Kroll, who is himself the subject of at least  29 complaints, has also  chided the Obama administration for its “oppression of police,” and praised President Trump as someone who “put the handcuffs on the criminals instead of us.”
title: 警察工会是如何成为改革努力的强大反对者的
title_en: How Police Unions Became Such Powerful Opponents to Reform Efforts

路透旧金山8月23日电-Facebook(Facebook Inc.)周四表示，该公司正在加大寻找与“Boogaloo”相关的用户群体的难度，“Boogaloo”指的是潜在的美国内战或文明崩溃。
世界上最大的社交媒体网络Facebook的一位女发言人说，Facebook将不再向类似协会的成员推荐这样的群组。
根据美国联邦调查局(FBI)的一份刑事起诉书，周三被控在拉斯维加斯反种族主义抗议活动中密谋暴力的三名男子中，至少有两人参加了Facebook上的Boogaloo群组。
Facebook表示，它在本周早些时候做出了这些改变。“考虑到可能使用暴力的讨论，我们觉得有必要采取这一行动，”这位不愿透露姓名的女发言人说。
研究人员和媒体今年发布的一系列报告引起了人们对这种松散运动及其在社交媒体上传播的关注。4月，一个名为技术透明项目的倡导组织警告说，Boogaloo的追随者正在讨论拿起武器，同时推动抗议活动，将各州从冠状病毒的限制中“解放出来”。
5月1日，Facebook禁止在附带武器图片和行动呼吁(如准备冲突)时使用Boogaloo及相关术语。
该公司表示，Facebook在其同名平台和Instagram部门上反对的具体条款正在演变。为了逃避审查，一些Boogaloo组织转而使用“Big Igloo”或“Big Luau”等术语，同时保持对武器、未来战争和阴谋论的讨论不变。
研究人员说，许多Boogaloo参与者认同白人民族主义团体或民兵。其他人是持枪权倡导者，或者反对他们认为政府越权的行为，一些人甚至支持针对警察暴行的黑人生命也是抗议活动。
Facebook表示，它调查的Boogaloo成员的政治从右到左“范围都有”。
周三被指控的人之一斯蒂芬·帕肖尔(Stephen Parshire)曾公开“喜欢”几个以Boogaloo为主题的群，他的个人主页显示，直到Facebook在案件立案后关闭了它。他还发布了一张南部邦联战旗的照片。
note_en:
SAN FRANCISCO (Reuters) - Facebook Inc is making it harder to find user groups associated with the term “Boogaloo,” which refers to a potential U.S. civil war or the collapse of civilization, the company said on Thursday.
  Facebook will no longer recommend such groups to members of similar associations, a spokeswoman for the world’s largest social media network said.
 At least two of three men charged on Wednesday with plotting violence at a Las Vegas anti-racism protest participated in Boogaloo groups on Facebook, according to an FBI criminal complaint.
 Facebook said it made the changes early in the week. “We felt we needed to take this action given discussions of potential use of violence,” the spokeswoman said on condition she not be named.
 A series of reports this year by researchers and media have drawn attention to the loose movement and its propagation on social media. In April, an advocacy group called the Tech Transparency Project warned that Boogaloo followers were discussing taking up arms while promoting protests to “liberate” states from coronavirus restrictions.
 On May 1, Facebook banned the use of Boogaloo and related terms when they accompany pictures of weapons and calls to action, such as preparing for conflict.
 The specific terms Facebook is acting against on its namesake platform and Instagram unit are evolving, the company said. To evade scrutiny, some Boogaloo groups have switched to terms such as “Big Igloo” or “Big Luau” while maintaining the same discussions about weaponry, future wars and conspiracy theories.
 Many Boogaloo participants identify with white nationalist groups or militias, researchers say. Others are gun-rights advocates or oppose what they see as government overreach and some even support Black Lives Matter protests against police brutality.
 Facebook said the politics of Boogaloo members it looked into “ran the gamut” from right to left.
 One of those charged Wednesday, Stephen Parshall, had publicly “liked” several Boogaloo-themed groups, his personal page showed until Facebook closed it after the case was filed. He had also posted a picture of a Confederate battle flag.
title: Facebook在抗议活动中限制“Boogaloo”群的传播
title_en: Facebook limits spread of 'Boogaloo' groups amid protests

可见电流建立了这个移动的LED装置，它使流动的电荷在导线上是可见的。如果你能看到电在移动，会不会更容易理解？做100安培电缆的方法非常简单。全世界的物理教师看到这一点，都会说，丝状的静电风流与这些由高速高压空气组成的奇怪线条一起玩耍。(不！#34；#34；#34；#*)。旋转磁盘装置使电磁场看得见，这是揭示看不见的场模式的危险旋转机制的想法。书，还有一个网站。保罗·F&39；的电路模拟器做了我一直梦想的事情：使用电荷流的概念来制作复杂电路的动画，而不需要任何硬件。慢速电荷继续移动，而导线表面的电荷显示为变化的红色和绿色！自20世纪80年代以来，K12教科书的供应一直在改善。1986年，当我第一次开始写这些文章时，我找不到不充满错误和误导性概念的文本。但是在我给马萨诸塞州的老师们讲课的时候，已故的史密斯学院(也是马萨诸塞州)的梅尔·斯坦伯格博士正在研究一个解决方案：Genecon发电机和超级电容器。他的C.A.S.T.L.E.高中课程，电容辅助教学和学习电学系统，旨在直接攻击典型的学生对电学的误解。教材：“中学电力与电路设计科学技术概念”，史密森/美国国家科学院STC/MS项目。

VISIBLE CURRENT Build this moving-LEDs device which makes the flowing charges visible in wires. Wouldn't "electricity" be easier to understand if you could SEE it moving?  Incredibly easy way tomake a 100-amp cable. Around the world, the physics teachers look at this and say"Doh!"  Threadlike streams of electrostatic wind Play with these strange "threads" of high-speed, high-voltage air.  Rotating diskdevice makes e-fields visible Idea for dangerous whirling mechanisms which reveal invisible fieldpatterns.    Books, but also one website. Paul F's circuit simulator does what I've always dreamed about: uses the "charge flow" concept to animate a complex circuit without needing any  hardware. The slowcharges move along, and the wires' surface charge appears as changing red and green!  Since the 1980s, K12 textbook offerings have been improving. When I first started these articles in 1986 I could find no text that wasn't choked with mistakes and misleading concepts. But during the time I lectured on the problem to teachers in MA, the late Dr. Mel Steinberg at Smith College (also in MA) was working on a solution: Genecon generators and supercapacitors. His C.A.S.T.L.E. highschool curriculum, Capacitor-aided System for Teaching and Learning Electricity, is designed to directly attack typical student misconceptions in electricity.
